content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
  (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
1/ 
Reed Gallogly 
City Attorney 
City of Santa Cruz 
PO Box 481 
Santa Cruz, CA 95061-0481 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-073 
Dear Mr. Gallogly: 
April 11, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Cruz Planning 
Commissioner Mark Mesiti-Miller regarding his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibiti9ns such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does Commissioner Mesiti-Miller have a financial interest in Mesiti-Miller Engineering 
(MME)? 
2. Does Commissioner Mesiti-Miller have a conflict of interest in the review of a project 
where MME was hired to provide engineering services on the project? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Yes. Based on the promised income due from MME, MME is a source of income and a 
financial interest of the planning commissioner. 
2. If a source of income to the commissioner (such as MME) is explicitly involved in a 
decision, you will be required to disqualify from making, participating in making, or influencing the 
decision. Without a specific decision we cannot provide formal advice concerning this question. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-073 
Page No. 2 
Currently, Mark Mesiti-Miller is serving as a Planning Commissioner in the City of Santa 
Cruz. In Santa Cruz, Planning Commissioners are charged with both making recommendations to 
City Council and with some decision-making authority. 
In March of 2015, Mark sold his entire interest in a company, Mesiti-Miller Engineering, 
Inc. ("MME"). Mark personally financed a portion of the purchase price by accepting a promissory 
note, payable by MME and personally guaranteed by the buyers. Under this promissory note, MME 
owes Mark approximately $4,471.20 per month until the sum of $200,000 (with interest) is paid off. 
A community member recently asked Mark if hiring MME to do work on a project, which is 
subject to review by the Santa Cruz Planning Commission, would result in Mark being "conflicted 
out" when the project came up for review. 
On April 6, 2017, you provided the following additional information: 
• There is no pending project, and the City is unsure whether MME would appear on behalf of 
an applicant. 
• It is expected the nature of the project would be residential or commercial development. 
• It is expected the MME would have completed its work when the project is presented, but it 
is further possible MME would be expected to or required to revise some work in response 
to comments from the Commission. 
• The City cannot opine as to whether MME would gain or lose future work depending on 
project approval-it would depend on MME's professional reputation. As no project is 
currently pending, the City cannot opine as to the size of the project in relation to other 
MME projects. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the public official's interests. 
Section 87103 also sets forth the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise under 
the Act. Those pertinent to your question are: 
• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(a).) 


File No. A-17-073 
Page No. 3 
Under Section 82034, an "investment" includes any financial interest in a debt 
instrument. In the Holzhaus Advice Letter, No. I-14-218, we said that an official had an 
investment interest in his former employer where the employer purchased the official's 
stock with a five-year note because a note is a debt instrument. (See also, Gore Advice 
Letter, No. A-15-071, applying the rule to a promissory note.) 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in 
the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official 
status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the 
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 
87103(c).) 
"For purposes of the Act, a public official's income includes income which as been 
promised to the public official at the time he or she has a legally enforceable right to the 
promised income." (Regulation 18703.J(a).) Thus, receipt ofloan repayments and 
interest are included in the definition of"income" for purposes of the Act's disclosure 
requirements. (Section 82030; Cahill Advice Letter, No. 76-122; Deddeh Advice Letter, 
No. 88-180.) The debtor on the loan would normally be considered a source of income 
to the official for the full amount of the loan until the loan is repaid and for twelve 
months thereafter. (Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-96-241.) 
According to the facts you have provided, the Commissioner received income for the sale of 
his interest in MME in March of 2015. The Commissioner has received future promised income 
based on the promissory note from MME. MME will be a source of income until 12 months after 
the promissory note is satisfied. In addition, the Commissioner will continue to have a separate 
investment interest in the company until the promissory note is cleared. 
We cannot conduct a full analysis of the applicability of the Act's conflict ofinterest 
provisions without consideration of a particular governmental decision and the related facts. Since 
there is no pending project, we can only provide the following general guidance with respect to the 
remainder of the analysis. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. An interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or 
subject of, the decision. (Regulation 1870l(a), emphasis added.) Similarly, Regulation 18702.3 sets 
forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a 
source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(l) provides: "The source is a claimant, applicant, 
respondent, contracting party, or is otherwise named or identified as the subject of the 
proceeding . . .. " 
We have said in the past that where a source of income will be identified in the applicant's 
materials presented to the city council and will appear on behalf of the applicants at the hearing, 
they would be considered explicitly involved. For example, in the Houston Advice Letter, No. 1-13-
031, we stated: 


File No. A-17-073 
Page No. 4 
"Where the governmental decision involves a project of a LSA client [LSA 
was the employer of the official] and LSA has been or will be hired as a 
consultant or sub-consultant for the project...LSA is directly involved in the 
decision. In these circumstances, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect on 
Councilmember Petros' economic interests in LSA is presumed to be material, 
and Councilmember Petros is prohibited from making, participating in making, 
or using his official position to influence the decision." 
Finally, as we noted above, since the Commission's advice is the application of the law (in 
this case, the Act} to a specific set of facts, the ultimate conclusion with respect to foreseeability 
and materiality cannot be made without the consideration of a specific decision and the related 
facts. Therefore, you should contact us for further advice if an application that MME worked on 
comes before the Planning Commission. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
. Wallace 
t General Counsel 
JWW:jgl
